words: This conversation with GLEN GILDER took place at his home not far from the
Spring Valley Reservoir outside of Troy, Idaho on December 9, 1976. His wife,
AGNES GILDER, also took part in the conversation. The interviewer was SAM
SCHRAGER.
SAM SCHRAGER: Were they active when you were kids?
GLEN GILDER: Oh, very. Yes, when I was a kid, they were. You see, we had
a good organization in Deary, and they had one here in Troy, but the
membership just kept dwindling, dwindling, dwindling. In Deary we
were down to seven active members Not enough.We argued for two
years and finally we went in with Potlatch. And Troy argued for
quite a period and they went all to Moscow. So, all the Odd Fellows
here in town go to Moscow once in a while. Al Peterson and Monty Helm
and, oh, quite a number. Arch Fiedler.

speaker: SS
words: When did you first get interested in them?

speaker: GG
words: Well, when I was a kid, I was pretty young, I was all fixed up to

speaker: GG
words: join the Odd Fellows along about 1919 with another group of young

speaker: GG
words: guys, and somehow or other, I got a job clear out of the country and

speaker: GG
words: had to go take it, so I didn't get in on that bunch and I didn t

speaker: GG
words: join until about 1965, probably, before Ijoined 'em. And, Iwouldn't

speaker: GG
words: a joined 'em then but Carl Lancaster and Henry Jones and Dwight Cory-

speaker: GG
words: Well, Carl and Henry were to go in with me in that first bunch and

speaker: GG
words: then they made up their minds, by golly, that they were going to get

speaker: GG
words: me in there and they did. But 1920 to '65 before I did it, now, those

speaker: GG
words: fellows had been in the lodge all that time. I see in one of the

speaker: GG
words: last papers where Eunice Jones was given a fifty-year pin at Potlatch.

speaker: GG
words: Well, Edna had more years than that in it. I'll bet you that Edna-

speaker: SS
words: Butterfield?

speaker: GG
words: Uh-huh. I'll bet you that they joined long before the teens. I'll

speaker: GG
words: bet you they joined up that lodge in-

speaker: GG
words: AGNES GILDER: Well, didn't it say in that paper?

speaker: GG
words: I don't think it did.

speaker: GG
words: Was it a big, major thing in the early years? Was it a really big

speaker: GG
words: organization?

speaker: GG
words: How did it compare to like the Grange?

speaker: GG
words: Much more percentage- volume- much more percentage and the Grange

speaker: GG
words: does now. Take a little place like Princeton; back during the early

speaker: GG
words: times that Grange had around seventy members, and it dwindled down,

speaker: GG
words: down, down and they went in with Potlatch.

speaker: GG
words: Potlatch?

speaker: GG
words: Uh-huh. Princeton went to Potlatch.

speaker: GG
words: In the Grange?

speaker: GG
words: No, in the Odd Fellows. Yeah, it was strong, not only here, but all

speaker: GG
words: over the United States. Had a tremendous membership. Fact it was

speaker: GG
words: anA-Vht-Red Men

speaker: GG
words: Woodmen, Masons and the Odd Fellows. And I think the Odd Fellows

speaker: GG
words: were the highest of all, but I think there was times for long periods

speaker: GG
words: when they were second. The Red Men, I think, have completely quit.

speaker: GG
words: The Woodmen of the World, I think have quit and the Odd Fellows are

speaker: GG
words: probably second or third place.

speaker: GG
words: The Odd Fellows, it was a lot bigger than the Grange at the time,

speaker: GG
words: wasn't it? When you were growing up. I mean around here.

speaker: GG
words: I so. The Grange was pretty popular, too. Well, a big percen

speaker: GG
words: tage of the Odd Fellows were Grangers. Same then as they are now.

speaker: GG
words: Here in Troy, there's Alfred and Art and Monty- they're all Odd Fel

speaker: GG
words: lows.

speaker: GG
words: There's a lot of overlap between the two.

speaker: GG
words: Yep. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: GG
words: Is there much difference between the Odd Fellows and like the

speaker: GG
words: Masons? As a group?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, they have different policies. I don't know but very little about

speaker: GG
words: the Masons, but their purposes have always been pretty parallel, I

speaker: GG
words: believe. Fellowship to benefit everybody involved in it, all they

speaker: GG
words: could.

speaker: SS
words: How old were you when you started working out?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, I don't know, about twelve.

speaker: SS
words: What was the first work that you did out?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, driving horses for somebody, I suppose. Yeah, I think that's it.

speaker: GG
words: It was just natural for me to drive horses; be around horses as any

speaker: GG
words: thing could be. When I first started hauling wheat from Kennedy Ford

speaker: GG
words: into Palouse for my uncle and granddad, I could no more lift a sack

speaker: GG
words: of wheat than I could fly. Somebody else had to load me and unload

speaker: GG
words: me, but I could drive the horses; four of 'em. And I didn't take a

speaker: GG
words: backseat there from any of the big fellows, either. And then from

speaker: GG
words: there I went to driving- oh, one of the first jobs I had was driving

speaker: GG
words: a grader team. On a highway grader from Harvard to the Hoodoos. Old

speaker: GG
words: Duf used to come get me every spring and take me up with him and I d

speaker: GG
words: stay up there two weeks and we'd fix all the culverts and clean out

speaker: GG
words: the ditches and grade the roads, and I'd go home and have about- oh,

speaker: GG
words: maybe fifty dollars. Didn't make much in days. About two dol

speaker: GG
words: lars and a half a day.

speaker: SS
words: Did you quit school by that time?

speaker: GG
words: No. If I'd need to I'd take a week off. (Chuckles) go back

speaker: GG
words: and I was going to catch it up, but I don't think I ever did. It

speaker: GG
words: was nothing uncommon. If you got a job, go do it. Lots of times

speaker: GG
words: we could get a job for driving a team and wagon putting gravel on the

speaker: GG
words: road, you know, in the fall of the year; we could get a couple of

speaker: GG
words: week's work. All we had to do was drive a team back and forth. Some-

speaker: GG
words: body else loaded us andsomebody helped us unload with dumpboards.

speaker: GG
words: That's what I would sooner done, and that's what I did.

speaker: SS
words: You mean sooner do than sit in a classroom?

speaker: GG
words: If I was sittin' in a classroom- I don't know for sure, but I think

speaker: GG
words: I must have spent a lot of my time lookin' out the window! I don't

speaker: GG
words: know, but I think I must have.

speaker: SS
words: Was it true for you that- now, I've heard a couple of people say that

speaker: SS
words: to do a man's work, was really what they wanted to do as a boy, to be

speaker: SS
words: able to do a man's work, was what they were looking forward to.

speaker: SS
words: Would you say that was true with you?

speaker: GG
words: Yeah, it was true. To compete with a man was the heighth of our am

speaker: GG
words: bition. If we would be lucky and could keep up with him, why we

speaker: GG
words: thought we was pretty big. If we could catch one a little older or

speaker: GG
words: a little akward and could surpass him at something, then we had the

speaker: GG
words: swellhead. Take two kids out shocking hay with a pitchfork, and

speaker: GG
words: they'd race from morning til night;try to get that row done quicker

speaker: GG
words: than the other guy. I worked one year for and he had me

speaker: GG
words: shocking and she'd come out in the field pretty early in the morning

speaker: GG
words: after she got the housework done, and her and I would race til noon.

speaker: GG
words: And she'd go in and get dinner and after she got the dishes done and

speaker: GG
words: all that, why, she'd be right back out there and doggone, if we didn't

speaker: GG
words: race then til she had to go milk. Laugh and make fun of each other

speaker: GG
words: like a couple of kids! She was forty, forty-five years old when I

speaker: GG
words: was about seventeen or eighteen. She worked awful hard. But they

speaker: GG
words: made it.

speaker: SS
words: Was that pretty common to do like what she did - for the women to

speaker: SS
words: get out and work when they were done with their housework?

speaker: GG
words: Yes, it was really common; the women got out and helped. Sometimes

speaker: GG
words: they elminated the hired man altogether. Many, many, many of 'em.

speaker: GG
words: They could drive horses as good as a man. Wives and daughters they

speaker: GG
words: right out in the field.

speaker: AG
words: Yeah, but they didn't work so long.

speaker: GG
words: Huh?

speaker: AG
words: They didn't , so long.

speaker: GG
words: I don't know, Amelia's still going good.

speaker: AG
words: Amelia who?

speaker: GG
words: Lindsey.

speaker: AG
words: She wasn't the last time I saw her, which is about fifteen years ago.

speaker: GG
words: She wasn't going real good, but then she's still agoin'.

speaker: SS
words: Do you figure that that was pretty - rough on the women to do that

speaker: SS
words: then? Agnes, is that what you're thinking?

speaker: AG
words: Yes, I do. They didn't get over it so good, may have looked like it

speaker: AG
words: but they didn't. She worked her head off, that's for sure and paid

speaker: AG
words: for it.

speaker: GG
words: Her big trouble is, she got awful heavy; way overweight.

speaker: SS
words: When she was still working?

speaker: GG
words: No, no, when she was still working she was right down to good workin'

speaker: GG
words: order, I think. I think she weighed around a hundred and thirty, or

speaker: GG
words: forty, fifty; somewheres around there. But now, she's over two hun

speaker: GG
words: dred.

speaker: AG
words: Well, I didn't know her when she was younger. When she was aboutwell,

speaker: AG
words: her kids were about five or six years old, she had a hired girl, too, that she helped

speaker: AG
words: and then went out in the field.

speaker: GG
words: Well, she did after they got well started and established. She had

speaker: GG
words: Genevieve and Naomi and some of them, but for a number of years, she

speaker: GG
words: just didn't. But she wasn't alone in that. All neighbors; the

speaker: GG
words: Hansons, the Hallgrens and all those people right close to them,

speaker: GG
words: it was all the same thing. The Gregorys.

speaker: SS
words: Was it just necessity that dictated it; that that's what a woman would

speaker: SS
words: do? Or was it more trying to get ahead?

speaker: AG
words: Quite a bit of it was necessity, because times weren't a great deal

speaker: AG
words: better than they are in this day and age. Might have had less money

speaker: AG
words: but it pime just as hard as it did in the last few years.

speaker: GG
words: Well, they had to sell a couple of hogs, a calf or something to pay

speaker: GG
words: us fellows our wages for the help they got in harvesting and haying.

speaker: GG
words: Money was hard to get. It wasn't easy. And they all were starving;

speaker: GG
words: they had places to pay for and outfits to pay for and living to make.

speaker: AG
words: It's one thing though, in those days, young men had a chance to get

speaker: AG
words: a better start than they have any chance of having now.

speaker: GG
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: AG
words: Of course roughly a hundred and fifty thousand now and now maybe

speaker: AG
words: fifteen thousand was taking care of the whole deal in those days.

speaker: AG
words: Pretty hard to get.

speaker: SS
words: It's funny that there's a lot less opportunity now than there used

speaker: SS
words: to be. Think there'd be a way that you could still compete.

speaker: AG
words: Well, up until this last year it has been possible, but boy, I don't

speaker: AG
words: think anybody can get ahead now. They're awful lucky if they are

speaker: AG
words: able to come out even.

speaker: GG
words: before 1928, if a man had a hundred dollars he could start far

speaker: GG
words: ming in a good shape.

speaker: SS
words: A hundred dollars?

speaker: GG
words: We rented eighty acres and I had what I'd made in harvest and Harold

speaker: GG
words: had an equal amount and we bought a Ford car with it. And we went

speaker: GG
words: to work in the mill, and every time we'd get a paycheck we'd buy a

speaker: GG
words: horse or calf or a harness or a plow or some damn thing, and we got

speaker: GG
words: started. We didn't have any cash. Every time we could make a dol

speaker: GG
words: lar we just applied it to some little thing that'd probably grow.

speaker: GG
words: And most of it did.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't have to spend much of that money just to live on at that

speaker: SS
words: time?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, no, eggs and beans and spuds were cheap. That's about all we

speaker: GG
words: knew how to cook; we was baching before we was married.

speaker: SS
words: Now is this your place you had at the time?

speaker: GG
words: No, we rented our eighty acres from my grandmother and got a start

speaker: GG
words: there. Did pretty good in '27, '28 and '29. Made some pretty good

speaker: GG
words: headway, and then the Depression come and we went the other way.

speaker: SS
words: What was the deal with your grandmother? Was it a certain amount of

speaker: SS
words: money you paid, or what?

speaker: GG
words: Well, we give her $125 a year for that place. She was tickled to

speaker: GG
words: death to get it; just for rent.

speaker: SS
words: Did you grow a crop on it?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, you bet, a good one. Our first crop on that place- forty acres-

speaker: GG
words: I think it was only about twenty-eight acres in wheat, but it made

speaker: GG
words: forty-two bushel. And we got two dollars and fifteen cents a bushel.

speaker: GG
words: And that was making darn good money. But, had years of

speaker: GG
words: that and then the Depression hit and wheat was worth twenty-five cents

speaker: GG
words: a bushel. That was a different story. That wouldn't pay for any

speaker: GG
words: part of it.

speaker: SS
words: What about the stock that you say you were buying? Were you raising

speaker: SS
words: them up then? Said you'd buy a pig or-?

speaker: GG
words: Yes, we did. We'd buy little pigs, little calves. And they grow and

speaker: GG
words: actually that was all the gain we made was about what they grew, but

speaker: GG
words: even that was pretty good for that time. (Chuckles) Oh, it's a dif

speaker: GG
words: ferent world, I don't know. I asked a lady in Wyoming last fall what

speaker: GG
words: would it cost to start ranching over there, and she told me that it

speaker: GG
words: would cost $1,000,000. they could get ahold of a lease- if they

speaker: GG
words: could get ahold of the land that is the home ranch and then to get

speaker: GG
words: ahold of a lease and keep it up, keep up their water rights, and stock

speaker: GG
words: enough to stock it and the machinery enough to take care of it, that

speaker: GG
words: that's about what it would cost. A young guy can't start unless you

speaker: GG
words: got a Dad or a Granddad with money to put him on the place. And it's

speaker: GG
words: the same in wheat.

speaker: SS
words: It doesn't sound like it was easy, though, even in the '20's when you

speaker: SS
words: were doing it. It certainly seems like not an easy haul to build up.

speaker: GG
words: It wasn't. But we were talking it over before we leased this place

speaker: GG
words: and said if we could get a job in the mill, which we thought we could;

speaker: GG
words: that was eight hours, but it wouldn't hurt us to go home and do chores

speaker: GG
words: after those eight hours anymore than it would if we drove a team on

speaker: GG
words: a plow all day and then went in and done chores in the same way. No,

speaker: GG
words: it wasn't easy; Long hours, and hard work. It could have been a lot

speaker: GG
words: of going without, but we were young and didn't know enough to go with

speaker: GG
words: out anything. If we wanted something, why, we went and done it.

speaker: SS
words: Well, will give me an instance of what you'd do instead of go without?

speaker: GG
words: Take our girl to the show or go to a dance. It was probably a couple

speaker: GG
words: of dollars, and now it would cost you twenty-five. We didn't go with

speaker: GG
words: out, because we didn't- they didn't have things like they've got 'em

speaker: GG
words: now. The only thing you could do was go to a show or go to a dance

speaker: GG
words: or go to a party in somebody's house; that's all there was. Couple

speaker: GG
words: of dollars was plenty for a weekend. But that couple of dollars

speaker: GG
words: was so big then that I don't know the twenty-five is any bigger

speaker: GG
words: now than that was. I don't know. Don't think so.

speaker: SS
words: Were you really holding down two jobs at once when you were doing

speaker: SS
words: that?

speaker: GG
words: Yes. That's exactly right. I had some plowing to do after I home

speaker: GG
words: from the mill at three o'clock, if I was on the dayshift, I'd go

speaker: GG
words: out and plow til six or seven. And that's the way I got all the

speaker: GG
words: work done;1 after work, do it. It was a small place and by figuring

speaker: GG
words: and scheming taking turns. And another thing, we had fairly

speaker: GG
words: good horses. I traded with my uncle, he put my crop in and then take

speaker: GG
words: my horses over to his place for the summer. Well, that's all I had

speaker: GG
words: to do as far as putting in my crop in was to buy the seed. And we

speaker: GG
words: did that one year. I did the work, but I don t think I did the

speaker: GG
words: thinking.

speaker: SS
words: Scheme and work and did a lot of thinking. Why didn't you do the

speaker: SS
words: thinking? I don't know what you mean. You mean, you didn't think

speaker: SS
words: about it enough?

speaker: GG
words: Well, yeah Well, I don't know, you had to scheme and plan and

speaker: GG
words: wonder how the devil are you going to make it. What are you going

speaker: GG
words: to do to make this come out right? What are you going to do to make

speaker: GG
words: that come out right? I didn't always hit it right. I missed it a

speaker: GG
words: few times, but that's what I mean by thinking. I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: Were there real choices that you had to make? You could have gone

speaker: SS
words: two or three different ways all the time?

speaker: GG
words: Well, you could have invested in different ways in the stock, or you

speaker: GG
words: could have lunged in and hit it or you could have in and missed

speaker: GG
words: it. You know, things then were like they are now; cattle could be

speaker: GG
words: a good price this year, hogs could be a good price this year, which

speaker: GG
words: they were in those first two years, in '27, '28 and '29, why, hogs

speaker: GG
words: were clear up to thirteen cents a pound. In 1930 and '31 they were

speaker: GG
words: between two and three cents a pound. I had some of both.

speaker: SS
words: Well, is that thinking or is that just rolling the dice? Seems to

speaker: SS
words: me like it's maybe a matter of luck.

speaker: GG
words: Yes, this rolling the dice- any time that you start farming, you

speaker: GG
words: start rolling the dice, and you never quit rolling the dice until

speaker: GG
words: you quit farming. There isn't any way to quit. Sometimes you can

speaker: GG
words: guess ahead, but, it's not all head work either, it's an equal amount

speaker: GG
words: of pure luck.

speaker: SS
words: How long did it take you to decide that you'd be better off getting

speaker: SS
words: out of that place?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, after three years it was going so darn good we thought we'd

speaker: GG
words: get a little bigger. We did. We got a little bigger and put so much

speaker: GG
words: produce on the market I guess that's why hogs went from thirteen

speaker: GG
words: cents to two. 'Cause everybody went with us. Oh, you always- reach

speaker: GG
words: ing out trying to boost yourself; lift yourself up another notch.

speaker: GG
words: And sometimes you do it; sometimes you didn't. When wheat was twen

speaker: GG
words: ty-five cents a bushel, a sack would hold two bushel and a half, and

speaker: GG
words: it cost us thirty cents to get the sack. And I think it cost ten

speaker: GG
words: cents a sack to get it threshed; that's forty cents. And your seed

speaker: GG
words: had cost you around two dollars an acre, and your work and your horse

speaker: GG
words: work went into it and finished out the crop. But, even so, that

speaker: GG
words: wasn't enough money to break even. When that depression come, we did

speaker: GG
words: have a few cows to milk- fairly good ones- and by working out after

speaker: GG
words: my crop was in or during the winter, if I could get work during the

speaker: GG
words: winter, I'd take it. But I generally couldn't. If I could cut around

speaker: GG
words: three quarters of a cord of wood a day, that was enough to eat

speaker: GG
words: on. For ten years, that's about what we did. We ate. Never had any

speaker: GG
words: or relief or WPA; we didn't have it. We could have got it, I pre

speaker: GG
words: sume. I never tried, I didn't want it. I don't blame the guys that

speaker: GG
words: did get it. I think it was, more power to 'em.

speaker: SS
words: So, really then, it was just holding on, not trying to- there wasn't

speaker: SS
words: a chance of getting ahead during that time.

speaker: GG
words: There wasn't. There wasn't any. The only way you possibly

speaker: GG
words: gone ahead, and some did, they'd acquire more land or more equipment

speaker: GG
words: and then when the prices broke back to a dollar a bushel, why, then

speaker: GG
words: they went right ahead. They had it made. But guys like myself didn't

speaker: GG
words: have the guts or foresight or whatever it took to do that. Didn't

speaker: GG
words: do it. When it did come up, we went ahead, alright, after it came,

speaker: GG
words: but we went awful slow.

speaker: SS
words: But you didn't own the land to start with, right? I mean, you didn't

speaker: SS
words: have anybody give you, or you didn't inherit it or you didn't have

speaker: SS
words: the money to buy when you were a young man, right? I mean, that

speaker: SS
words: seems to put you in a different position to start with over some of

speaker: SS
words: these guys that had land,

speaker: GG
words: Well, we did finally buy that place up at Harvard, and we went five

speaker: GG
words: thousand dollars in debt. Didn't we?

speaker: AG
words: I don't remember.

speaker: GG
words: We lost it.

speaker: SS
words: When did you buy it?

speaker: GG
words: '34, wasn't it? '35?

speaker: AG
words: '34. I think, I don't remember.

speaker: SS
words: You'd just been married a pretty short time then, right?

speaker: AG
words: Six years?

speaker: GG
words: Well, wheat was thirty cents then; on the way up. Got up to sixty

speaker: GG
words: cents, finally. Still wasn't enough because we were back on payments

speaker: GG
words: and we were back on interest- our taxes were kept up but you couldn't

speaker: GG
words: pay anybody if you owed somebody, you couldn't pay 'em. So the only

speaker: GG
words: thing to do was just not owe 'em. Go without.

speaker: SS
words: How long did you keep that place?

speaker: GG
words: Til 1939. Yeah, '39. We had our walking papers, and then we came

speaker: GG
words: over here. Still hard sledding, it wasn't easy going. I think wheat

speaker: GG
words: was seventy-five cents then. I think cream was- what? About- clear

speaker: GG
words: up to fifteen cents a pound.

speaker: AG
words: Eleven cents;

speaker: GG
words: Well, eleven cents for some of it, but I think it had kinda gained

speaker: GG
words: a little by the time we got here. Then the war come on. But even

speaker: GG
words: that didn't make any difference to us for- oh, what?- it took two or

speaker: GG
words: three years for that to affect us here very much.

speaker: AG
words: Didn't affect anybody but the ones that had it already paid for til

speaker: AG
words: for a pretty long time.

speaker: SS
words: Why-

speaker: AG
words: Prices didn't come up. Prices didn't come up for two or three years

speaker: AG
words: after the war.

speaker: GG
words: Slow starting, but they did eventually start. But during the war all

speaker: GG
words: the neighbors around here pulled out and went to work for war effort.

speaker: AG
words: Ship yards and Boeing.

speaker: SS
words: Lot of people did that, huh?

speaker: GG
words: You bet. Sheltons,Pelfreys, Davises, Fountains, Bensons.

speaker: SS
words: Did they go to Portland, Mostly? Seattle? Portland;Mostly?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, no, they went to Bayview and went to every place. Any place

speaker: GG
words: there was war efforts. One of 'em went to work up there at the hos

speaker: GG
words: pital on construction work.

speaker: AG
words: The shipyards and Boeing were the main

speaker: SS
words: They took their families or just the men?

speaker: AG
words: Most 'em did, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Kept their places, though?

speaker: AG
words: Some of 'em did and some of them didn't.

speaker: GG
words: Most of 'em didn't.

speaker: SS
words: Really?

speaker: AG
